segments.s513-g17: This is a past-tense clause in which the subject 'همه دشمنان' (all the enemies) is followed by the object phrase 'کام دل' (literally 'the heart's desire'). 'یافتند' is the simple past (3rd person plural) of یافتن used here in the sense 'obtained' rather than merely 'found', so the phrase means they obtained/fulfilled their heart's desire.
segments.s513-g18: This clause has the past verb 'رسیدند' (3rd person plural) meaning 'they reached/attained' and is followed by 'جایی که ...' (the place that/where), a relative clause introduced by 'که' which links to 'بشتافتند' ('they hurried') describing that place. The relative 'که' here means 'which/that'.
segments.s513-g19: 'بمابر' is an archaic/poetic prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'for us' (or 'to us'); it is followed by the temporal adverb 'کنون' (now). 'تلخ گردد' uses the present/subjunctive form 'گردد' (= گردد/گشت) meaning 'becomes' or 'turns (bitter)'; 'جهان' here denotes life/the world as the patient that becomes bitter.
segments.s513-g20: The verb 'شویم' is the present subjunctive/optative first-person plural of شدن ('to become') meaning 'let us become' or 'we become.' 'خروشان' is a predicative adjective/adverb ('tumultuous, roaring') placed before the verb in poetic word order. 'آشکار و نهان' are two adverbial/adjunct words 'openly and secretly/hidden' coordinated by 'و', qualifying the manner of becoming.
segments.s513-g21: 'چنین' ('thus') modifies the speech verb. 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (here listed senses do not include the verbal sense, so index is -1). 'قیصر' is the subject 'the Caesar.' 'به آوای نرم' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'in a soft/gentle voice'; 'به' marks the manner ('in/by'), 'آوای' is the construct (voice of) and 'نرم' modifies it.
segments.s513-g22: The particle 'که' introduces the content of the speech ('that' / 'namely'). 'ترسنده باشید' is an exhortation using the present subjunctive/imperative form 'باشید' (2nd person plural) with the adjective 'ترسنده' meaning 'fearful'/'timorous' (i.e., be cautious). 'با' governs the instruments 'رای و شرم' ('counsel and modesty'), indicating one should be fearful (cautious) accompanied by counsel and shame/modesty.
segments.s513-g23: 'ز' (poetic variant of از) here means 'from' or 'regarding.' 'اندرز من' is a possessive phrase 'my counsel/advice.' 'سربسر' is an adverb meaning 'entirely' or 'throughout.' 'مگذرید' is a present/subjunctive/imperative plural 'do not let pass/do not disregard' — together the clause is a negative injunction not to ignore the speaker's advice.
segments.s513-g24: 'چو' here means 'as' or 'as you wish' (often used idiomatically as 'do as you will'). 'خواهید' is 2nd person plural present of خواستن meaning 'you wish/you will.' 'کز' introduces a subordinate clause 'that/from which,' linking to the consequence. 'جان و تن' are coordinated nouns 'life and body.' 'برخورید' (3rd person plural or subjunctive 2nd/3rd) literally 'to meet/encounter'; used here in sense 'suffer/meet (loss, death)' — i.e., you will incur loss of life and body if you act as you please.
segments.s513-g49: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. زان دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from that plain' (زان = 'from that' before noun), with the object before the verb as is common in classical Persian. برداشتند is the past tense, 3rd person plural 'they lifted/removed'. The whole segment means 'when they lifted the coffin from that plain.'
segments.s513-g50: همه is the subject 'everyone'. دست بر دست is an idiomatic doubled noun phrase 'hand upon hand' (دست + بر + دست) functioning adverbially/figuratively; بگذاشتند is past 3rd plural 'they placed/put'. Together it reads literally 'everyone put hand on hand' (i.e. folded/laid their hands).
segments.s513-g51: دو آواز شد is an impersonal/experiential construction: 'two voices arose' or 'there were two voices' (شد here = 'arose/happened'). رومی و پارسی follows as the two agents indicated (a Rumi and a Parsi) — in effect 'a Roman and a Persian spoke.'
segments.s513-g52: سخنشان is سخن + possessive suffix -شان 'their speech/words'. ز تابوت is the prepositional phrase 'about/from the coffin' (ز = 'from/about' in poetic usage). بد یک بسی is an adverbial predicate phrase: بد 'bad/ill' qualifying the speech and یک بسی ('a little / to some extent') giving degree — together 'their words about the coffin were somewhat ill/unsuitable.'
segments.s513-g53: هرانکس که ... بود is a relative clause 'whoever (it was) that ... was'. او پارسی بود = 'he was Persian' with او as the personal pronoun for emphasis; گفت is the main verb 'said' (here used as the reporting verb after the relative clause). Word order places the relative phrase before the reporting verb.
segments.s513-g54: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. او را is the patient marked with the definite object marker را. جز ... ایدر = 'except here' (جز = 'except', ایدر = 'here'). نباید نهفت is a periphrastic negative obligation: نباید 'ought not / must not' + نهفت (infinitive/verb 'to bury/hide' in classical usage) — 'he ought not to be buried except here.'
segments.s513-g55: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ایدر بود = 'was/had been here' (ایدر 'here', بود past 'was'). خاک شاهنشهان is a genitive construction 'the dust/earth of the shahanshahs' (شاهنشهان following خاک to show possession). Altogether: 'When the emperors' dust lay here...'.
segments.s513-g56: چه is an exclamatory/rhetorical 'what / why' introducing a question; تازند is present 3rd plural of تازیدن 'to rush/haste/drive about' used here rhetorically about moving the coffin. تابوت گرد جهان literally 'the coffin around the world' (گرد + noun = 'around X'). The sentence asks, in effect, 'why rush/carry the coffin around the world?'
segments.s513-g33: زدند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb (they struck/they set), here part of the light compound آتش زدند 'they set fire'. اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/within', and سرای نشست is a noun phrase 'the hall/house of the sitting/assembly' (نشست as noun 'session/assembly'); together the clause reads 'they set fire inside the assembly‑hall'.
segments.s513-g34: هزار اسپ را is the quantified object 'a thousand horses' plus the definite/object marker را; دم is the part affected (here 'tip/edge' used for 'tail' in context), and بریدند is the past plural 'they cut'. پست is a postpositive adjective/adverb meaning 'low/base' modifying the violent action. The clause therefore means 'they cut off the tails of a thousand horses'.
segments.s513-g35: نهاده is a perfect/past participle used as a past action 'they placed/put'; بر اسپان is the prepositional phrase 'upon the horses' (اسپان = horses), and نگونسار زین is a descriptive phrase 'woeful/mournful saddle' (نگونسار qualifying زین). The whole clause: 'they placed woeful saddles on the horses.'
segments.s513-g36: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'; گفتی here functions as 'you said' (past 2nd person) introducing reported speech or comment — the supplied sense list did not include 'to say', so that entry is marked -1. همی is the classical emphatic/continuous particle ('indeed/was'), and برخروشد زمین is a verb phrase meaning 'the earth rose/was moved' (برخروشد as a verbal clause). Altogether: 'you said, indeed the earth rose.'
segments.s513-g37: ببردند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they took/carried away'; صندوق زرین is the object 'the golden chest' (noun + adjective); به دشت is a directional phrase 'to/into the plain'. The clause reads 'they carried the golden chest to the plain.'
segments.s513-g38: همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle ('continually'/'indeed') modifying the following verb; ناله is the subject 'a wail/complaint', از آسمان gives the source 'from the sky', and برگذشت is a past verb 'passed by/rose up'. The clause means 'a wailing continually passed from the sky.'
segments.s513-g39: سکوبا is a proper name or designation (agent) — here treated as the washer/cupbearer; بشستش is the verb 'he washed him/it' with the enclitic object -ش 'him'; به روشن گلاب is the instrumental phrase 'with clear/pure rosewater' (به used here to mark the means). The clause: 'Sakuba washed him with clear rosewater.'
segments.s513-g40: پراگند is a verb or verbal adjective meaning 'scattered/was scattered' used here as a transitive action 'they scattered'; بر تنش is the prepositional phrase 'on his body' (تن 'body' + clitic ش 'his'); کافور ناب is the noun phrase 'pure camphor' (کافور = camphor, ناب = pure). Together: 'they scattered pure camphor upon his body.'
segments.s513-g25: پس از من is a prepositional phrase 'after me' (پس here = 'after') placed at the start to set the temporal frame. شما را uses the object/beneficiary marker را after شما to indicate 'for you' / 'to you' (emphatic/accusative). همینست کار is a compact, poetic clause: همینست (contraction of همین است) 'this is exactly' + کار 'the affair/lot', so literally 'this is your lot.'
segments.s513-g26: نه is the sentential negation ('not') negating the following phrase. با من is a prepositional phrase 'toward/with me' (i.e. 'to me'); همی is the archaic progressive/continuous particle marking ongoing action. کند is the classical third-person singular present/past-like verb 'does/makes' and روزگار at the end is the subject ('fate'/'the world') placed postverbally for emphasis: literally 'Fate does not do ill to me.'
segments.s513-g27: بگفت is the simple past 'he said.' The sequence 'بگفت این و' links the quoted/previous remark into the narrative. جانش is the possessed noun 'his life' (جان + possessive -ش) and برآمد 'arose/issued forth' with ز تن 'from the body' is an idiomatic verbless clause meaning 'his life departed his body' (i.e. he died).
segments.s513-g28: شد is the past/preterite 'became' (or 'was made'). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the' pointing to the person just described. The sequence نامور شاه لشکرشکن is a nominal phrase with epithets: نامور 'renowned' modifying شاه 'king', and لشکرشکن 'army‑breaker' as a compound epithet; the whole means 'he became that famed, army‑breaking king.'
segments.s513-g29: ز لشکر سراسر is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the army, throughout' (ز = 'from/out of', سراسر = 'throughout/entire'), placed before the verb for vivid effect. برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' — together: 'a roar arose throughout the army.'
segments.s513-g30: ز فریاد لشکر is another ablative/genitive phrase 'from the army's shout' (ز = 'from', فریاد + لشکر = 'the army's cry'). بدرید is a past third‑person plural 'they tore' (here used figuratively) and گوش is the object 'ear(s)'; word order is verb + object: 'the army's shouting tore the ears.'
segments.s513-g31: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject; خاک بر سر is an idiomatic object phrase 'dust upon (their) heads' and همی is the archaic progressive particle ('were' / continuous aspect). بیختند here is a past plural verb meaning 'they threw/scattered/poured' (poetic for 'they cast dust on their heads'); the verb is 3rd person plural past. Note: the provided sense options for بیختند do not match this verbal use, so the sense index below is set to -1.
segments.s513-g32: ز مژگان is an ablative phrase 'from (their) eyelashes' (a poetic image for tears). همی marks continuous aspect; خون دل is a genitive/compound 'blood of the heart' (i.e. inner grief) and ریختند is the past third‑person plural 'they poured'/'they shed' — together: 'from their eyelashes they poured the blood of their hearts' (a poetic way of saying they wept bitterly).
segments.s513-g57: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative formula 'thus said'; گفت is the 3rd-person past tense verb 'said'. The following words Rumi (رومی) + yeki (یکی) + rahnamā (رهنمای) form an inverted noun phrase 'a certain Roman guide' (one who is a Roman and a guide), with یکی as an indefinite 'a certain' modifying رهنمای. Poetic word order places the verb phrase before the subject.
segments.s513-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that…'. ایدر is the archaic/poetic adverb 'here'. نهفتن is the infinitive/masdar 'to hide' used nominally as the action; ورا is the object clitic 'him' (to him); نیست is the stative 'is not/there is not'; رای is the noun 'counsel/plan'. Word order is somewhat inverted: 'نهفتن ورا' (to hide him) functions as the idea which 'رای نیست' (no counsel exists for).
segments.s513-g59: اگر introduces a conditional. بشنوید is 2nd-person plural/polite present-subjunctive ('you hear' / 'you should hear') used in the protasis 'if you hear'. آنچ 'that which' is a relative/nominalizer referring to the reported content; گویم is the 1st-person present subjunctive 'I say' (dependent clause 'that I say'); درست is an adverb 'correctly/exactly' modifying the whole clause. The clause order places the conditional verb before its object phrase, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s513-g60: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. در آن خاک is a prepositional phrase 'in that earth/soil' (در + demonstrative آن + خاک). ریزد is a 3rd-person singular verbal form (here used poetically to mean 'lies/is laid/was placed' or 'is buried') governing the clause. که introduces a relative clause modifying خاک; رست is a past-state verb ('was/ceased/was delivered' in classical usage) describing what happened there. Overall: 'Alexander is laid in that soil in which he… (perished/was changed).' Poetic diction allows terse verb forms and inverted clause order.
segments.s513-g61: یکی پارسی نیز is 'a certain Persian also' (یکی = 'a certain', پارسی = 'Persian', نیز = 'also/likewise'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech / these words' (this remark). The sequence is typical poetic inversion: the speaker and adverb come before گفت and the object follows.
segments.s513-g62: که‌گر is the fused conditional particle meaning 'if' (که + گر). چندگویی is a noun meaning 'much speaking / talkativeness' (the act of speaking many words). نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive 'does not come/does not reach' (3rd person). به بن is a prepositional phrase 'to the root' (به = to; بن = root/foundation), so the clause reads 'if much is spoken, it does not come to the root (i.e., it doesn't reach the essence)'.
segments.s513-g63: نمایم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'I show / I will show' (classical/poetic usage). شما را marks the indirect/direct object 'to you / you' with the object marker را. یکی مرغزار is the object 'a certain meadow' (یکی = 'a certain', مرغزار = 'meadow'). The verb comes first (نمایم) followed by the object, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s513-g64: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'of/from', introducing a genitive relation: 'a memorial of/from the kings and predecessors.' شاهان (kings) and پیشینگان (predecessors/former generations) are coordinated by و, and یادگار is the head noun 'memento / memorial' summarizing what the meadow is. The phrase therefore qualifies the meadow as a memorial to past kings and elders.
segments.s513-g9: ز بیماری او is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of his illness' (ز = because of + بیمار‌ی = illness + او = his). غمی شد سپاه uses شد as a past 'become' verb: سپاه (the army) is the subject that 'became' grief‑stricken; the order is slightly inverted (cause first, then the result with the verb).
segments.s513-g10: که introduces a result/relative clause ('so that'). بی‌رنگ is a predicative adjective 'colorless/pale' placed before the verb. دیدند is simple past plural 'they saw' (the subject 'they' refers back to سپاه), and رخسار شاه (‘the king’s countenance’) is the object/NP seen — classical word order places the object phrase after the verb here.
segments.s513-g11: همه دشت = 'the whole plain' (همه modifying دشت). یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely' modifying خروشان ('roaring/tumultuous'). شدند is past plural 'became' — the plural verb agrees with the collective sense of همه.
segments.s513-g12: چو here functions as a comparative/temporal 'as/when'. بر آتش = 'upon the fire' (بر + آتش). تیز جوشان is a compound descriptive sequence: تیز (literally 'sharp', used poetically for fierce/hot) modifies جوشان ('boiling/seething'). شدند repeats the past plural 'became' and continues the comparison with the same subject (the plain).
segments.s513-g13: همی before a past verb gives a habitual/continuous sense ('kept saying'). گفت is the past 'said' and هرکس is the subject 'everyone'. که begins the subordinate content ('that') and بد روزگار is an evaluative phrase 'an ill (unfavorable) time' or 'bad fate'.
segments.s513-g14: که again introduces a subordinate clause 'that…'. از رومیان marks the source/agental cause ('because of/by the Romans'); کم شود is a classical present/subjunctive use of شود expressing an eventuality ('will be reduced' / 'become less'); شهریار after the verb is the noun affected (the king who will be diminished) — classical Persian often places the verb before the affected noun.
segments.s513-g15: فرازآمد is a compound past verb 'rose up/appeared' (poetic). آن گردش بخت is a noun phrase 'that turn/cycle of fortune' with شوم qualifying it as 'ill‑omened' or unlucky; here شوم is an adjective ('ill‑omened').
segments.s513-g16: که introduces the result clause. ویران شود is a subordinate verb phrase in the present/subjunctive sense 'will be ruined/laid waste'. زین ('by/from the saddle') functions as an instrumental/agental phrase (i.e. by the cavalry's saddles — a poetic way to mean mounted troops), سپس = 'thereafter/thereupon', and مرز روم = 'the Roman frontier' is the domain affected; note the verb precedes the affected noun, a common classical ordering.
segments.s513-g41: This is a past-action clause built around the verb کردش (کرد + ـش enclitic). ز introduces the material/source ('from' — a poetic preposition) followed by the noun phrase دیبای زربفت ('a brocade woven with gold' — زربفت postmodifies دیبای). کردش is the past tense of کردن with the enclitic ـش = 'him', so 'they made for him' and کفن is the direct object 'a shroud'.
segments.s513-g42: This is a verbless descriptive phrase giving the state of the assembly. خروشان functions as an attributive/adverbial 'tumultuous/roaring' modifying انجمن ('assembly'). بران here is an old/poetic locative/demonstrative ('for/on that' = بر آن) linking انجمن to شهریار ('that king'). Word order places the descriptive word before the head noun.
segments.s513-g43: تن نامور is a noun + epithet ('the renowned body'); Persian classical word order commonly places the epithet after the noun. زیر introduces the locative phrase 'under/beneath' and is followed by the noun phrase دیبای چین, where چین ('fold') postmodifies دیبای ('brocade') to mean 'folds of brocade'.
segments.s513-g44: نهادند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' (they placed). The particle تا here marks extent/limit ('as far as / so that to the point that'), introducing the following locative phrase پای در انگبین ('foot/feet in nectar'), so the clause means they placed him with his feet in nectar. انگبین is a lofty poetic word for nectar/ambrosia.
segments.s513-g45: سر تنگ تابوت is a noun phrase where سر ('top/lid') is modified by the adjective تنگ ('narrow/tight') and followed by تابوت ('coffin'), effectively 'the tight lid of the coffin'. کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' and سخت here functions adverbially ('firmly/tightly'), so the clause means 'they made the coffin's lid tight' / 'they closed the lid tightly'.
segments.s513-g46: شد is the simple past/copular 'became'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' and the following compound noun phrase سایه‌گستر دلاور درخت ('shade-spreading, brave tree') is the predicate that follows شد: 'that shade-spreading brave tree became (firm)'. Note the classical inversion where the predicate noun phrase follows the copula.
segments.s513-g47: نمانی is a form of ماندن ('to remain') with the negative particle n- preserved in classical usage: here it functions as 'you will not remain' (2nd person); همی is a continuous/aspectual particle ('still/indeed') giving iterative or emphatic force. در introduces the locative phrase 'in the dwelling of سپنج' (سپنج = 'sponge' — i.e., the porous/sponge-like house), so the clause means 'you will not remain in the sponge-like house'.
segments.s513-g48: This is a pair of parallel exclamatory fragments introduced by چه ('what' / exclamative). Each fragment has the pattern 'چه [X] به [Y]' — here the first is 'چه یازی به تخت' and the second 'چه نازی به گنج'. The meaning is rhetorical: 'what use/boast of the throne, and what pride of treasure.' Several lexical items (یازی, نازی) are archaic/poetic here meaning 'boast/claim' or 'pride', so the phrase condemns boasting about throne or treasure.
segments.s513-g1: چو is the poetic temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a past-tense periphrasis 'became aware' (شد = became); لشکر is the subject 'the army' placed after the verb by poetic inversion; از درد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'of/about the king's pain' that gives the content of that awareness.
segments.s513-g2: جهان گشت is a simple past/resultative 'the world became/turned'; بر here marks the relation 'for/toward' (an older usage), نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/illustrious ones', and سیاه is a predicative adjective meaning 'dark/gloomy' (metaphorical, signifying grief).
segments.s513-g3: نهادند is the past plural 'they placed/laid' (verb). به تخت بزرگی is a directional phrase 'toward/onto a great throne' and روی is the noun 'face' (direct object) — in this poetic order the direction comes before the thing whose face was turned/placed, so the whole means roughly 'they set/turned his face toward a great throne.'
segments.s513-g4: جهان شد is 'the world became'; سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely'; پر از is the idiom 'full of' and governs the following noun گفت‌وگوی 'conversation/talk', so the clause means 'the world was completely filled with talk.'
segments.s513-g5: چو ... آگاه شد is again a temporal clause 'when ... became aware'; سکندر is the subject 'Alexander' and stands before the clause for emphasis; از لشکر is 'from the army' (source of information).
segments.s513-g6: بدانست is a past-tense verb 'he knew/realized'. کش here is an archaic noun meaning roughly 'span/extent' (not covered by the provided senses, so rendered as the span of life); روز کوتاه شد is a clause 'the day(s) became short' i.e. 'his days were shortened/near their end' — together: 'he realized that the span of his days had grown short.'
segments.s513-g7: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('so that' / 'that'), and تخت بیرون برند is the content of the order: بیرون 'out' and برند is the present-subjunctive/plural form used after such verbs to mean 'they should carry/take (it) out.'
segments.s513-g8: This continues the command: از ایوان شاهی 'from the royal iwan' (source/place), به هامون 'to the plain' (destination), and برند repeats the verb 'they should carry/bring' — a straightforward from... to... motion construction.
segments.s513-g73: برفتند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb ‘they departed/ set off.’ پویان is a participial/noun (the pursuers/seekers) appearing after the verb for poetic word‑order. به کردار غرم is a prepositional phrase ‘in the manner/conduct of wrath’ where کردار = ‘manner/conduct’ and غرم = ‘wrath/anger’; the whole phrase modifies how they departed.
segments.s513-g74: بدان here functions as ‘to that’ (b- + demonstrative), pointing to the grove just mentioned. بیشه‌کش is a compound of بیشه ‘grove’ + کش (agentive) = ‘one who works/attacks the grove’ (a hunter/raider); باز in this context is likely the noun ‘baz’ = ‘hawk/falcon’ or an appositive describing the hunter as a falconer. خوانند is present/past narrative 3rd‑pl. ‘they call/summon,’ and جرم is the term/name they give (the line is terse/poetic and the exact sense of جرم here is obscure).
segments.s513-g75: بگفتند is the narrative past ‘they said’ (used to introduce speech). پاسخ ('answer/reply') is the noun object, چنین ‘thus/so’ modifies how the answer was given, and داد is the past verb ‘gave.’ باز here is the adverb ‘back/again’ meaning the reply was returned or given in reply.
segments.s513-g76: که introduces the reported question (that / ‘saying’). تابوت شاهان is a genitival phrase ‘the coffin/coffers of the kings.’ چه دارید راز is an interrogative phrase: چه = ‘what,’ دارید = 2nd‑person plural/polite ‘you have,’ راز = ‘secret/mystery’ — together: ‘What secret do you have?’
segments.s513-g77: که introduces the clause. خاک سکندر is ‘the dust/earth of Alexander.’ به اسکندریست is an archaic way of saying ‘is to/for Alexandria’ — i.e. ‘belongs to Alexandria’ or more freely ‘is in Alexandria;’ اسکندریست is an adjectival/relational form meaning ‘Alexandrian.’
segments.s513-g78: کجا here is rhetorical (‘how/what’), followed by کرده (perfect/participial use of ‘do/make’) to form ‘what has (been) done/what befell.’ بد روزگاری is an abbreviated noun phrase ‘ill fate / bad time,’ and که زیست is a relative clause ‘that he lived’ — together: ‘what ill fate he underwent while he lived.’
segments.s513-g79: چو = ‘when’; آواز is the object ‘the cry/sound’; بشنید is past ‘(they) heard’ (classical perfective), and لشکر is the subject ‘the host/army.’ برفت is an archaic past of ‘رفت’ meaning ‘set off / went away’ — here the verb agrees with the (collective) subject ‘لشکر.’
segments.s513-g80: ببردند is past 3rd‑pl. ‘they took / they carried away.’ زان = ‘from that’ (ز + آن) points back to the grove; بیشه صندوق = ‘the chest/box of the grove’ (object). تفت at the end is lexically obscure here — in the given sense list it normally means ‘to scorch/roast,’ but in context it likely functions as an adjectival or corrupt/poetic word; the line is terse and the precise role of تفت is ambiguous.
segments.s513-g65: This clause is past narration: ورا is the clitic/object 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis; جرم is a predicative accusative ('guilt' / 'an offence') and خواند is a past third‑person verb meaning 'called' (here 'called [someone] guilty' / 'accused'). جهاندیده پیر is the agent phrase 'the worldly‑wise old man' that follows the verb in poetic word order (subject after verb).
segments.s513-g66: بدو is the allative/clitic 'to him' (repeating or reinforcing the beneficiary), اندرون بیشه و آبگیر is an adverbial phrase 'inside the grove and (the) pond' with اندرون 'within' governing the following nouns; و simply joins the two nouns.
segments.s513-g67: چو is a temporal/conditional 'when/if'; پرسی is the verb 'ask' in a 2nd‑person form (here 'you ask'); the clitic ت is the 2nd‑person object/marker; را is the definite object marker attached to the following object; پاسـخ آید is a verb phrase 'an answer comes' with پاسـخ ('answer') placed before آید ('comes') — a common object‑before‑verb ordering in classical/poetic Persian; ز کوه 'from the mountain' is a source adjunct.
segments.s513-g68: که introduces a result or relative clause 'such that/that'; آواز او ('his voice') is the object of بشنود; بشنود is a present/subjunctive form of 'hear' (here used to express what will happen) and هر گروه ('every company') is the noun phrase that means 'each group/party' — note the inverted/poetic ordering with the verb before or adjacent to the subject.
segments.s513-g69: بیارید is an imperative/plural command 'bring' (poetic/older form), with مر the clitic 'to me' immediately after the verb, and پیر فرتوت را the definite direct object 'the aged old man' marked by را. The object follows the verb and is made definite by را as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s513-g70: هم 'also' (adds this instruction), ایدر 'here' is an adverbial specifying place, بدارید is a plural imperative 'hold/keep' (from داشتن used as a causative/imperative in older usage), and تابوت را is the definite object 'the coffin' with را. Word order places the adverb before the verb and the object after it.
segments.s513-g71: بپرسید is an imperative/plural 'ask' (here used as a command); اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; کوه is the subject 'the mountain'; پاسخ دهد is a subordinate clause 'gives an answer' where دهد is the present/subjunctive/remote-future form 'gives' (classical Persian often uses present-subjunctive for future or potential actions).
segments.s513-g72: شما را marks 'you' as the beneficiary/affected party (را emphasizing the object); ب is the shortened prepositional prefix 'به' meaning 'by/with'; دین here is part of the phrase 'بدین رای' (poetic contraction of 'به این رای' = 'in this way') but the listed lemma senses do not match that function (so treat it as the fossilized element 'this' in context); رای is 'plan/way' and فرخ is the noun/adjective 'good fortune' used as the thing bestowed; نهد is a present tense verb 'sets/places' (here 'bestows'). The clause thus reads 'it will, by this counsel/way, place good fortune upon you.'

sentences.s513-s9: All the enemies obtained the desire of their hearts; they reached the place to which they had hurried.
sentences.s513-s10: For us now the world becomes bitter; let us become tumultuous, openly and in secret.
sentences.s513-s11: Thus the Caesar said in a gentle voice: 'Be fearful with counsel and modesty.'
sentences.s513-s12: Do not wholly disregard my counsel; do as you will, but (you will) meet loss of life and body.
sentences.s513-s25: When they lifted the coffin from that plain, everyone folded their hands.
sentences.s513-s26: Two voices arose — a Rumi and a Parsi — and their speech about the coffin was somewhat poor.
sentences.s513-s27: Whoever among them was Persian said that he must not be buried anywhere except here.
sentences.s513-s28: When here lies the dust of the shahanshahs, why should the coffin be carried about the world?
sentences.s513-s17: They set fire inside the meeting‑hall and cut off the tails of a thousand horses.
sentences.s513-s18: They put woeful saddles on the horses; as you said, the earth rose.
sentences.s513-s19: They carried the golden chest into the plain; a lamentation passed from the sky.
sentences.s513-s20: Sakuba washed him with clear rosewater and scattered pure camphor upon his body.
sentences.s513-s13: After I am gone, this will be your lot; fate will not treat me badly.
sentences.s513-s14: He said this, and his life left his body; he became that famed, army‑breaking king.
sentences.s513-s15: A cry rose throughout the whole host; the army's shouting split the ears.
sentences.s513-s16: They all cast dust upon their heads; from their eyelashes they poured the blood of their hearts.
sentences.s513-s29: Thus said a certain Roman guide: that here there is no counsel to hide him.
sentences.s513-s30: If you hear exactly what I say, Alexander lies in that earth where he perished.
sentences.s513-s31: A certain Persian also spoke this saying: 'If you speak many words, it will not reach the root.'
sentences.s513-s32: I will show you a certain meadow, a memorial of kings and of former generations.
sentences.s513-s5: The army was filled with grief over his illness, so that they saw the king's countenance grown colorless.
sentences.s513-s6: The entire plain became tumultuous at once, as if seething over a fierce fire.
sentences.s513-s7: Everyone kept saying that it was an ill time, that because of the Romans the king would be diminished.
sentences.s513-s8: That ill‑omened turn of fortune rose up, so that thereafter the Roman frontier would be laid waste by the saddle.
sentences.s513-s21: They made a shroud from gold-brocade; the assembly for that king was tumultuous.
sentences.s513-s22: They laid the renowned body beneath folds of brocade, with its feet set in ambrosial nectar.
sentences.s513-s23: They closed the coffin's lid tightly; that shade-spreading, brave tree became firm.
sentences.s513-s24: You will not remain in the sponge-like dwelling; what boast of the throne, and what pride in treasure!
sentences.s513-s1: When the army learned of the king's pain, the world grew dark for the famed men.
sentences.s513-s2: They turned his face toward a great throne; the world became entirely full of talk.
sentences.s513-s3: When Alexander learned from the army, he perceived that the span of his days had grown short.
sentences.s513-s4: He commanded that they carry the throne outside, that they bring it from the royal iwan to the plain.
sentences.s513-s37: The pursuers set out in a wrathful fashion; to that grove they called back the grove‑hunter (the falconer), whom they named Jorm.
sentences.s513-s38: They said; the answer was returned thus: 'What secret have you concerning the coffin of the kings?'.
sentences.s513-s39: That Alexander’s dust is Alexandrian — what ill fortune befell him in the time that he lived?
sentences.s513-s40: When the cry was heard, the army set out; they carried away from that grove the chest.
sentences.s513-s33: The worldly‑wise old man called him guilty, and to him (said), "(enter) the inner grove and the pond."
sentences.s513-s34: When you ask, an answer will come from the mountain, so that every company hears his voice.
sentences.s513-s35: Bring to me the aged old man; also here keep the coffin.
sentences.s513-s36: Ask; if the mountain gives an answer, it will, by this counsel, place good fortune upon you.
